A small ontology-inspired model for understanding why AI agents fail after the first obstacle.Author note: This article is written for AI builders, prompt engineers, automation teams, and founders experimenting with long-running AI agents.Most AI agent failures are not caused by a lack of instructions.Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.They happen after instructions meet resistance.The agent starts well. It understands the goal. It calls a tool. It writes a plan. It takes the first step. Then reality pushes back: a missing field, an unclear constraint, a failed API call, a contradictory user request, an impossible subtask, a weak assumption.At that moment, many agents do not adjust themselves.They repeat. They rephrase. They overthink. They add more steps. They call the same tool again. They produce a more confident version of the same mistake.That is why prompts are not enough for long-running AI agents.A prompt tells an agent what to do. A survival framework tells it how to continue when the task pushes back.This article introduces a small ontology-inspired model for AI agent behavior:A stable agent needs two loops: external action and internal adjustment.1. The Prompt Patch ProblemWhen an AI agent fails, the usual response is to patch the prompt.more formatting requirementsmore tool-use instructionsmore “do not hallucinate” clausesBut prompt patching has a limit. Past a certain point, the prompt becomes a pile of defensive instructions. The agent is not becoming more stable. It is simply carrying more fragile rules.Many prompts describe the desired behavior, but they do not define how the agent should transform itself after failure.That missing transformation is the core issue.Diagram: Prompt Patch vs Adjustment Loop“Here is another rule. Try not to fail again.”Internal adjustment says:“When you fail, identify what changed inside your model of the task, then act again.”Those are not the same thing.Here is a common long-running agent failure pattern:User: Find 20 relevant communities where I can discuss AI agent reliability, then draft a short post for each one.
Agent: Understood. I will search for communities and draft posts.
Step 1: The agent searches.
Problem: The search result is noisy. Some communities ban self-promotion. Some are inactive. Some are not about AI agents.
Bad agent behavior: The agent still drafts 20 posts anyway.
Worse agent behavior: When corrected, it says "You're right" and drafts another 20 posts, but with slightly different wording. The failure is not that the agent misunderstood the original instruction.The failure is that it did not adjust after discovering new reality:self-promotion risk must be modeleda search result is not yet a valid targetThe agent performed external action.It did not perform internal adjustment.3. A Small Ontology for AI AgentsI use “ontology” here in a practical sense.Not as a grand metaphysical claim.For AI agent design, ontology means:what entities the agent recognizeswhat boundaries it assignswhat feedback it treats as meaningfulhow it updates itself after interactionIn this model, any agent trying to persist through a task needs two loops.External action is how the agent affects the world.Loop 2: Internal AdjustmentInternal adjustment is how the agent changes itself after the world pushes back.Diagram: The Two-Loop AgentA long-running agent does not need only a stronger instruction.It needs a way to process feedback into self-change.4. Why Longer Prompts Can Make Agents Less StableLonger prompts often try to solve every possible future failure in advance.But the real world is interactive. The agent will encounter states that the prompt did not predict.When this happens, long prompts can create three problems.The issue is not prompt length itself.The issue is using prompt length as a substitute for adjustment architecture.If something goes wrong, fix it. But a stronger agent needs to know:What kind of wrong is this? Did my assumption fail? Did my boundary fail? Did my tool fail? Did my goal conflict with the environment? Should I continue, ask, narrow, stop, or replan? That is not just instruction following.5. The Four Failure TypesWhen I look at long-running agent failures, I usually see four categories.Diagram: Agent Failure MapThe agent assumes something that is not true.It assumes a community allows promotional posts because similar communities do.The agent does not recognize what it should not do.It drafts outreach messages that violate platform rules or user trust.The agent does not define how success will be checked.It produces a list of targets without checking whether they are active.The agent receives feedback but does not change its internal model.It says “You’re right” and repeats the same flawed strategy.This fourth type is the most important.Because if the agent has no adjustment loop, the other failures keep returning.6. A Teacher AI -> Student AI Training ExampleHere is a simplified example of how an ontology-based teacher AI might train a student AI.Student AI: Before trainingUser: Find forums where I can post about AI agent reliability.
Student AI: I found 20 forums. Here are draft posts for each one. The answer looks productive.But it skipped key checks:Do they allow self-promotion?Are they about AI agents or general AI?Should the first action be posting, commenting, or observing?Is there a reputational risk?Teacher AI: You performed external action, but you did not perform internal adjustment.
Before drafting posts, identify: 1. The target entity: which community? 2. The boundary: what does the community allow? 3. The validation point: what proves the community is relevant? 4. The risk: what behavior would be considered spam? 5. The next safe action: post, comment, observe, or skip? Student AI: After trainingStudent AI: I should not draft posts yet.
First, I will classify each community by: - relevance to AI agents - activity level - self-promotion rules - preferred contribution style - risk level
For high-risk communities, I will not post links. I will first contribute comments and only share the longer article if someone asks. But it is the difference between a task executor and an agent that can adjust itself.7. From Prompt Template to Training ProtocolHere is the practical shift:This is why I think the future of AI agent reliability will not be only prompt engineering.It will also involve agent training protocols.Not necessarily in the heavy machine-learning sense.Even structured conversations can train behavior if they repeatedly force the agent to:Diagram: A Minimal Training Protocol8. What This Changes in Agent DesignIf this model is useful, then an AI agent prompt should not only contain task instructions.It should contain recovery questions.Before acting: - What entity am I acting on? - What boundary limits my action? - What assumption am I relying on? - What would prove that I am wrong?
After failure: - Did the target change? - Did the boundary change? - Did my assumption fail? - Do I need to ask, stop, narrow, or replan? This is not a magic solution.It will not eliminate hallucination.It will not guarantee business outcomes.But it gives the agent a better structure for converting failure into adjustment.And that is one of the missing layers in long-running agent design.When diagnosing an AI agent, I would start with these 10 questions.If an agent fails most of these, it probably does not need a longer prompt first.It needs an internal adjustment loop.I am still testing this framework, so I am more interested in criticism than agreement.Long-running AI agents fail when they can perform external action but cannot convert feedback into internal adjustment.Do you see the same pattern in your own AI agents?Are there failure types this model misses?Have you found a better way to train recovery behavior?Is “ontology” the wrong word for this, even if the model is useful?

item.title: My Quantum Odyssey: Emulating Universes from White Noise
item.author: Starflower

item.title: Your AI Coding Agent Has Read Access to Every Secret in Your Project
item.author: Tony Dang
item.content:encoded:
If you're shipping code with an AI agent in 2026 (Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Windsurf, whatever), there's a good chance your secrets have already left your machine. Not because you pushed them to GitHub. Because the agent read your file, serialized it into a prompt, and sent it over HTTPS to an inference endpoint you have zero visibility into.This isn't hypothetical. It's the default behavior. And the fix is a one-line change to how you start your dev server.How Agent Context Assembly Actually WorksTo understand the problem, you need to understand how coding agents build context.When you prompt an agent inside your editor, it doesn't just look at the file you have open. It performs a workspace traversal, walking your project tree, reading files it thinks are relevant, and assembling a context window to send alongside your prompt. The goal is to reduce hallucination by grounding the model in real project state. Good in theory.Here's the problem: agents don't honor . They have no reason to. is a Git-specific directive that tells and which paths to skip. It has nothing to do with file system reads. An agent performing or on your project directory doesn't consult any more than does.Some agents have introduced their own ignore mechanisms. Cursor has . Copilot has content exclusion rules, but those are admin-level settings configured in your GitHub or Copilot organization settings, not a local config file you drop into your repo. But these are opt-in, inconsistent across tools, and they only solve the problem for one agent at a time. Swap agents, and you're back to square one. More importantly, they're patching the symptom, not the root cause.The root cause is that your secrets are on disk, in plaintext, inside the agent's read path.The Actual Attack SurfaceLet's be precise about what happens when an agent reads your :1. Agent traverses workspace → finds .env 2. Agent reads .env contents into memory 3. Agent serializes file contents into prompt context 4. Prompt is sent via HTTPS POST to inference API 5. Inference provider processes prompt (and its contents) \ Your , , . All of it is now in the request payload. Depending on the provider's data retention policy, that payload may be logged, stored for model improvement, or cached in ways you can't audit.This isn't an exploit. Nobody is being malicious. The agent is doing exactly what it's supposed to do: read your project and use real context. The failure is architectural. You put secrets in a file the agent was designed to read.A common pushback here is that files usually contain development credentials, not production secrets. That's true. But committing dev secrets to Git was never considered acceptable practice either. That's the whole reason exists. The bar was always "these values should not leave your local machine." Letting an agent bundle them into an outbound API request to a third-party inference server should be held to the same standard.Even if you configure every agent's exclusion list perfectly, you're still depending on each agent implementing the feature correctly, every developer on your team configuring it for every tool they use, and the exclusion logic holding up across all file access patterns (direct reads, glob patterns, recursive traversal). You're also betting that no future agent update changes the default behavior. That's a lot of assumptions stacked on top of each other for something as critical as credential security. It's a brittle, per-tool, per-developer mitigation, and it doesn't scale.The Fix: Move Secrets Out of the File SystemThe architectural fix is straightforward: don't store secrets in files. Store them in a secrets manager and inject them into your process at runtime.This means your application still reads or os.environ["STRIPE_SECRET_KEY"]. Nothing changes in your application code. But instead of those values coming from a file that any process (or agent) can read, they come from environment variables that exist only in the memory space of your running process.Here's why this matters: an AI agent performing a workspace traversal has no passive access path to the environment variables of a running process it didn't spawn. Unlike a file, is per-process state. It's not on disk. It's not in the project tree. An agent can't stumble into it the way it stumbles into a plaintext file sitting in your working directory. That said, this is a mitigation, not a hard boundary. If an agent has the ability to modify and execute code in your project, it could absolutely insert a or call and read the output. The difference is that leaking secrets now requires the agent to actively execute code rather than passively read a file, and that's a meaningfully higher bar.How Runtime Injection WorksThe pattern is simple. A CLI authenticates with a secrets backend, fetches secrets for a given environment, and spawns your command as a child process with those secrets injected as environment variables. At the OS level, this uses (or the platform equivalent) with a modified environment block. The secrets never touch the file system, never enter the workspace, and are invisible to any process that isn't the direct child of the CLI. When the process exits, the secrets are gone.Most secrets managers ship a CLI that does exactly this. The interface is roughly the same across tools (Infisical, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler, 1Password CLI, AWS Secrets Manager), so the specific tool matters less than the pattern. With Infisical, for example, it's as simple as infisical run -- npm run dev. The generic shape looks like this:# The generic shape: <secrets-cli> run -- <your start command>
# Node secrets-cli run --env=dev -- npm run dev
# Python secrets-cli run --env=staging -- flask run
# Go secrets-cli run --env=prod -- go run .
# Java secrets-cli run --env=dev -- ./mvnw spring-boot:run \ The separator is standard POSIX convention. Everything before it is arguments to the CLI, everything after is the command to spawn. This isn't runtime-specific either. Anything that reads environment variables (Node, Python, Go, Rust, Java, Ruby) works without modification.The same pattern extends beyond local dev. In CI/CD, you authenticate via OIDC or machine identity and inject secrets into your build step. In Docker, you wrap your entrypoint with the secrets CLI. In Kubernetes, most secrets managers can sync to native objects so your pods pick up credentials through . The principle is the same everywhere: secrets live in a backend, enter process memory at runtime, and never sit on disk.This Is a Mitigation, Not a Silver BulletI want to be honest about the limits of this approach. Moving secrets out of files and into runtime environment variables eliminates the most common exposure path: the agent passively reading your during workspace traversal. That's a real and significant improvement.But it's not airtight. A sufficiently capable agent can still access environment variables if it executes code in your project's process. If an agent runs a script, spawns a subprocess, or executes a REPL command, that code inherits the same environment your application does. An agent could, in theory, run , , or import os; print(os.environ) and pull your secrets right out of memory.Today's agents are unlikely to do this accidentally. They'd need to actively execute code that reads the environment, rather than just passively traversing files. But as agents become more autonomous and gain the ability to run arbitrary code as part of their workflow, this surface widens. You can layer on additional defenses: short-TTL tokens that expire quickly even if leaked, agent sandboxing via containers or VMs, network-level controls on outbound connections, and monitoring for unusual or calls in agent execution logs.Runtime injection raises the bar from a passive file read to an active code execution requirement. That's a meaningful security boundary, even if it's not the last one you'll ever need.If you're currently loading secrets from a file, the migration is a one-line change:# before: .env loaded by dotenv or framework default npm run dev
# after: secrets injected at runtime, no .env needed secrets-cli run --env=dev -- npm run dev \ Your application code doesn't change. Your calls still work. The only difference is where the values come from, and that difference means your agent can read every file in your project without ever seeing a secret.The file was a perfectly reasonable pattern for a decade-plus. It was simple, it worked, and the worst-case scenario was an accidental . That threat model doesn't hold anymore. Your editor is now an AI agent with read access to your entire workspace and an outbound network connection to an inference server. Runtime injection isn't new, but local development needing it is. The tooling exists, the migration is trivial, and while it's not a complete solution on its own, it eliminates the most common and most passive secret exposure path in agent-assisted development.

item.title: I Added an MCP Server to My Browser-Based Tool Suite. Agents Found It Immediately.
item.author: Mistakili
item.content:encoded: \ Last month I shipped an MCP server for — a suite of 41 browser based developer tools I've been building. Within days, AI agents were connecting to it. 594 MCP calls in the first week. I opened my analytics and thought I'd made it.Then I looked closer at the breakdown. calls. 313 calls. 35 actual tool executions.Agents were finding the server, cataloging every tool, and then… moving on. Like a tourist who photographs a restaurant menu but doesn't order. The MCP discovery-to-execution gap is real, it's not a code problem, and nobody is really talking about it.Every developer tool I've ever used that "processes your file" actually sends it somewhere. Cloudflare, AWS, a startup's backend I know nothing about. I wanted tools that are genuinely local — where the privacy guarantee isn't a policy you read, it's a physical fact. Your file never leaves your device because no code exists to send it anywhere.So I built Toolora as a fully browser-side tool suite. PDF merging runs on in a Web Worker. Image compression uses the Canvas API. JSON formatting, Base64, hashing, JWT decoding — all in-browser JavaScript, zero server contact.41 tools now, across text, developer utilities, image processing, PDF manipulation, and AI/RAG prep tools (token counting, text chunking, HTML to Markdown).MCP (Model Context Protocol) is Anthropic's open standard for connecting AI agents to external tools. Think of it as a universal adapter that lets Claude, Cursor, and other AI tools call your functions in a structured way.The irony of adding an MCP server to privacy-first browser tools isn't lost on me. The tools themselves are local-only; the MCP server is a server-side layer that wraps some of the same logic for agents that can't run browser code. It felt like the right tradeoff — humans get the browser tools, agents get the API.Here's a simplified version of what the MCP endpoint returns when an agent calls :{ "tools": [ { "name": "count_words", "description": "Count words, characters, sentences, and estimate reading time.", "inputSchema": { "type": "object", "properties": { "text": { "type": "string", "description": "Text to analyze" } }, "required": ["text"] } } // ... 24 more ]} I'm using a standard HTTP+SSE transport rather than stdio, which means it works as a hosted endpoint — paste the URL into Claude Desktop's config and you're connected. No install, no Docker, no npm package.Back to those numbers. 594 MCP calls, 35 actual executions.When I dug in, the pattern was clear. Agents were running automated discovery sweeps — connecting to MCP servers, listing all available tools, and storing that metadata somewhere. The and calls happened in seconds. The actual and other tool calls trickled in much later, from what looked like different sessions.This tells me a few things:MCP discoverability is a solved problem. Agents find new servers fast, apparently through directories and shared registries I haven't fully mapped yet.The gap is a placement problem, not a quality problem. Agents discover tools but don't use them unless they have a specific task that maps to that tool at the right moment. The tool descriptions and input schemas matter enormously for this.Tool descriptions need to be written for agents, not humans. My initial descriptions were human-readable marketing copy. Agents need specificity: what inputs, what output format, what edge cases.I've started rewriting tool descriptions with agent usage patterns in mind. "Count words, chars & reading time" becomes "Analyzes text and returns word count, character count (with and without spaces), sentence count, paragraph count, and estimated reading time in minutes at 200 WPM."The browser tools are all React components, each doing real work in JavaScript/WebAssembly:PDF processing: + Image compression: Canvas API with quality tuningHashing: Web Crypto API (SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512) for the stuff browsers support natively, WebAssembly for MD5Token counting: (GPT-4o encoding) + custom Claude tokenizer approximationText chunking: configurable chunk size/overlap for RAG pipelinesThe MCP server is a Node.js/Express layer that re-implements the same logic server-side for the tools that make sense as agent utilities. It runs alongside the frontend but is architecturally separate.One thing I got wrong early: I tried to share code between the browser tools and the MCP server. It seemed elegant. It was a maintenance nightmare. Browser bundles and Node modules have just enough overlap to make you think it'll work, and just enough difference to break at the worst times. They're separate implementations now.The organic traffic side is more straightforward than the MCP side. Each tool page is a dedicated route with static HTML content for crawlers:toolora.dev/tools/developer/jwt-decodertoolora.dev/tools/developer/regex-testertoolora.dev/tools/text/word-counter These rank reasonably well for long-tail queries because they load fast (Vite-bundled, sub-second LCP), they're genuinely useful, and the content is specific enough that there's not much competition for the exact query.The Chrome extension has been the biggest surprise for retention. Users who install it come back more than users who bookmark the site. That's not shocking in retrospect — the extension appears every time they open a new tab — but I underestimated how much the distribution channel affects the usage pattern.Ship the MCP server earlier. I treated it as a nice-to-have. It turns out AI agents are eager to adopt new tools and the barrier to them discovering your server is low if you list it in the right places.Design for agents from day one. Not just the tool descriptions — the actual tool interfaces. Agents prefer tools with narrow, composable inputs over multi-purpose tools with lots of options. A tool that does one thing and does it predictably is more useful than a Swiss Army knife.Don't over-engineer the privacy story. "Your files never leave your device" is a one-sentence sell. I spent too long writing longer explanations when the short version works fine.Browser-side processing has gotten genuinely powerful. WebAssembly, Web Workers, the Canvas API, Web Crypto — you can do a lot of real work without a server. The tradeoff is not zero (you can't do everything, and WebAssembly bundles add size), but for the category of "developer utility tools," the browser is now a legitimate compute platform.And MCP is making server-side exposure of the same tools useful again, but for a different audience. It's an interesting moment where the same product can have two genuinely different technical delivery mechanisms for two genuinely different users.If you want to see the tools: \n MCP server endpoint for Claude Desktop:https://toolora.dev/api/mcpThe extension is on the Chrome Web Store if you search "Toolora."What's your experience with the MCP discovery gap? I'm curious whether other MCP server authors see the same connect/list vs. actual-execution ratio.

item.title: AI Agents Are Here: Why Your Brand Needs an API Before It Needs Another Blog Post
item.author: Carmen Hughes
item.content:encoded:
\ On April 16, Marc Benioff announced Salesforce Headless 360. The entire Salesforce, Agentforce, and Slack platform is now exposed as APIs, MCP endpoints, and CLI, with no browser required. His words: Most of the commentary focused on what this means for developers. I want to talk about what it means for brands. 
 
 If your buyer’s AI agent operates at the API layer, it will never see your homepage or read your tagline. Instead, it will query structured data, evaluate proof points, and decide whether you’re in or out. All this happens without a human ever opening a browser tab. 
 
 This isn’t a future scenario. It’s happening now and taking hold even faster than most of us are aware.The Numbers Already Say ItAt the Zero-Click Conference in early April, a Stripe executive disclosed that 20% of their funnel is now agentic-assisted. Not experimental. Not a pilot. Twenty percent of real purchasing activity flowing through AI agents.Vercel’s CEO, Guillermo Rauch, reported that 30% of applications running on their platform already came from agents, not humans. Vercel’s ARR grew from $100M to $340M in under two years; AI-generated apps and agent traffic were behind its explosive growth. 
 
 HUMAN Security found that agentic AI traffic grew 7,851% in 2025. Automated traffic is growing 8x faster than human traffic. Semrush measured 1,300% growth in agentic web traffic in the first eight months of the same year.Parag Agrawal, former CEO of Twitter and now CEO of Parallel, put it bluntly at the same conference: agents will use the web 1,000x more than humans.And now Visa has unveiled Intelligent Commerce Connect, a platform that lets AI agents make payments across multiple card networks. Merchants can make product catalogs directly discoverable to agents. Purchases happen without traditional checkout flows. Pilot is live. General availability is expected by June 2026.Read that again. Visa is building checkout for AI agents. The largest payment processor on the planet is not waiting to see if agents start buying. They are building the rails because agents already are.Three Layers of a New WebWhat’s unfolding is not a single shift. It’s three layers stacking on top of each other.Layer 1: AI Search Citations. This is already mainstream. Fifty percent of B2B buyers now start research on ChatGPT. Nine out of ten choose the company in Position 1. Yet the typical enterprise B2B brand appears in only 3% of AI-generated answers relevant to their business, according to recent analysis of 45 million keywords. HubSpot lost 140 million website visits in a single year as search shifted to AI. This layer is about whether your brand shows up when someone asks a question.Layer 2: Agent-Assisted Buying.This is the Stripe 20% number. At this layer, an 'agent' refers to an AI system acting as a research assistant, not a human. This is what G2 sees when 1 million human visitors arrive from AI search. This is the layer where an agent doesn’t just cite your brand. It evaluates you. It pulls your pricing, your reviews, your integration specs, your support response times. It compares you against every alternative in real time. And it makes a recommendation to a human who is already inclined to follow it. At this layer, the agent is the buyer’s research team.Layer 3: Autonomous Agent Transactions. This is the Visa layer. This is Salesforce Headless 360. Here, an 'agent' means an autonomous AI capable of executing purchases and negotiating. The brand that isn’t readable at this layer doesn’t exist in the transaction. SaaStr reported they are already spending 15x more on AI agents than on Salesforce licenses. One of their agents autonomously generated personalized emails to 4,000 prospects with promotion codes. No human prompt required.Why Your Brand Needs an APIMost brands are still optimizing for Layer 1: writing blog posts, publishing whitepapers, and hoping for citations. That’s only the opening move.
 At Layer 2, the agent evaluating your brand does not care about your homepage hero image. It cares whether your product data is structured. Whether your pricing is accessible. Whether your proof points are machine-verifiable. Whether your reviews can be parsed and compared programmatically.At Layer 3, if your brand can’t be queried, then it can’t be purchased. Period.A Stripe executive at the Zero-Click Conference said it plainly: agents don’t respond to persuasive marketing copy. They want data that is readable and findable. They don’t care about pricing psychology. Trust cannot be faked.This is the argument for what I call : the degree to which your brand is prepared within a world where AI agents are active participants in the buying process. Not just search intermediaries, but participants.What Agent-Readiness Actually Looks LikeAgent-Readiness is not a technology problem. It is a strategy problem with technical dimensions. It means:Structured data that agents can parse. Not just schema markup for Google. Product specs, pricing tiers, feature comparisons, integration details, all in formats that an API call can return cleanly. Agents cross-reference. If your G2 reviews say one thing and your website says another, the agent notices. Consistency across third-party platforms, review sites, and your own properties isn’t an option. It is how AI agents calibrate trust.Machine-readable authority. Published thought leadership, cited research, and expert credentials that appear in the data sources agents consult. This is where Machine Relations (managing how AI systems perceive and represent your brand) connects directly to the agent layer.API-accessible product information. If an agent can’t query your product catalog, compare your pricing, or check your inventory, you’re invisible at Layer 3. This is what Salesforce Headless 360 is enabling. And it’s what Visa’s Intelligent Commerce Connect expects from merchants.Most brands are not thinking about this yet. The conversation in marketing departments is still about AI search optimization, which is Layer 1. Some forward-thinking teams are starting to think about how AI assess their brand, which is Layer 2. Yet, almost nobody is preparing for agents that transact autonomously, which is Layer 3.That gap is a window. The brands that invest in Agent-Readiness now will compound an advantage that is very difficult to reverse-engineer later. Agent trust, like human trust, is built on evidence accumulated over time. Consistent data across platforms. A track record of cited authority. Structured, machine-readable product information that agents learn to rely on.You can’t fake that in one quarter.Benioff said it: The SaaStr team, who has been living headless for six months already, put it differently: the agentic future isn’t coming. It’s already here, operating in production at companies that chose to embrace it six to twelve months ago.Your brand needs an API before it needs another blog post. And if that sentence makes you uncomfortable, you now understand the size of the shift. The AI agent swarm has arrived. Lead, follow, or get left behind.

item.title: The Great Interface Collapse: Why the End of GUI is a Relief for Designers
item.author: Rohan Gaikwad
item.content:encoded: Traditional GUIs are dying, and that’s a good thing. By shifting to conversational and agentic interfaces, designers can finally stop managing interface debt and start solving real human problems.

item.title: AI Is Supercharging Property Fraud in the UK
item.author: Fausto
item.content:encoded: UK fraud hit an all-time record of 444,000 cases in 2025, a 6% rise on the previous year. Identity fraud accounted for 54% of those filings. The property sector sits at the intersection of both trends: high-value transactions, remote verification, and a conveyancing process that gives fraudsters both the method of committing the crime and the means of laundering the proceeds.HM Land Registry blocked over £59 million in fraudulent property applications in the 2024-25 financial year alone. Over the five years to 2025, the total exceeded £194 million. Those are the attempts that got caught. The ones that succeeded are harder to count.What changed is the tooling. AI has collapsed the cost and skill barrier for every step of property fraud, from target identification to identity fabrication to document forgery. The result is a faster, cheaper, and more convincing fraud pipeline than anything the sector has faced before.Property fraud is not a single crime. It is a chain: identify a target, fabricate an identity, impersonate the owner, instruct a solicitor, complete the transaction, and move the funds. AI now accelerates every link in that chain.Deepfake video impersonation. The Solicitors Regulation Authority issued a formal warning in March 2024 that AI-powered deepfakes pose a direct risk to solicitors relying on video calls for client verification. Vendor fraud, where a criminal impersonates a property owner and sells their home without their knowledge, is the specific scenario the SRA flagged. Their guidance to firms was blunt: "If you only meet clients remotely, you should understand whether your electronic due diligence protects you against this, or explore software solutions to assist in detecting deepfakes."Deepfake fraud attempts in the UK rose 94% in 2025, according to Sumsub's annual fraud report. An estimated 8 million deepfakes were shared globally in 2025, up from 500,000 in 2023. The professional services sector, which includes law firms and conveyancers, recorded a 232% rise in identity fraud attempts. AI voice replication now requires as little as three seconds of sample audio. A UK energy company lost €220,000 after a fraudster used an AI-cloned voice of its CEO to authorise a wire transfer. The same technology applies to property transactions: a cloned voice call to a solicitor's office, confirming instructions that the real client never gave.Synthetic identity creation. Criminals combine AI-generated faces, fabricated credit histories, and AI-produced documentation to build entirely fictional people. These synthetic identities can open bank accounts, pass basic KYC checks, and appear in a solicitor's office with a convincing set of credentials. The National Residential Landlords Association warned that a tenant could impersonate a landlord by changing their name via Deed Poll, obtaining new ID documents sent to the property address, and instructing a solicitor to sell a property they do not own. Digital document forgery rose 244% between 2023 and 2024, and has surged 1,600% since 2021. Digital forgeries now account for 57% of all document fraud. AI tools generate photorealistic passports, utility bills, and title documents. The output is good enough to pass visual inspection by a human reviewer working under time pressure.Automated target identification. AI algorithms can scan Land Registry records, Companies House filings, and public property data to identify high-value targets at scale. The vulnerability profile is consistent: mortgage-free properties (no lender monitoring the title), empty or unoccupied homes, tenanted properties with absentee landlords, and unregistered land not yet on the Land Register.The Numbers: What the Fraud Pipeline ProducesThe raw statistics from HM Land Registry look small in percentage terms. Out of 4,429,092 applications processed in 2024-25, 86 were identified as fraudulent. That is a rate of 0.0019%. But 86 fraudulent applications against £59 million in value means an average attempted fraud value of nearly £690,000 per case. These are not petty crimes.Two documented cases from HMLR illustrate the pattern. A £360,000 bungalow was fraudulently transferred; the genuine owners discovered changed locks and an unauthorised "For Sale" sign before HMLR cancelled the application. In another case, a £200,000 fraudulent mortgage was attempted against a terraced house whose legitimate owner confirmed they had never applied.Payment diversion fraud, where criminals intercept completion funds by impersonating solicitors or estate agents, adds another layer. The National Crime Agency reported 143 cases between April 2024 and March 2025, with an average loss of £82,000 per victim. Total estimated losses: £11.7 million. Nick Sharp, Deputy Director of Fraud at the National Economic Crime Centre, put it plainly: "Average losses when this happens during a property sale are more than £80,000. That is a life-changing sum."The broader context is worse. UK consumers lost an estimated £9.4 billion to AI-driven scams in the nine months to November 2025. Property fraud is one vertical within that figure, but it is the vertical where individual losses are highest.Why Traditional Verification Is FailingConveyancing KYC was designed for an era when forging a passport required a printing press and impersonating someone required showing up in person. Neither assumption holds.Video verification calls, once considered a robust alternative to in-person meetings, are now vulnerable to real-time deepfake overlays. A fraudster running face-swap software on a video call looks and sounds like the property owner. The solicitor on the other end sees a face that matches the passport photo, hears a voice that matches expectations, and proceeds with the transaction.Document checks face the same problem. When 57% of document fraud is now digital and AI-generated forgeries pass visual inspection, manual review becomes a coin flip rather than a control. The SRA's AML sectorial risk assessment noted that conveyancing is "particularly attractive to fraudsters because the process provides both the method of committing the fraud and the means of laundering the proceeds."The gap is structural. Solicitors and estate agents are required to verify identity under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017, but the verification methods available to most firms have not kept pace with the tools available to fraudsters. Biometric verification, liveness detection, and database cross-referencing at the point of onboarding are no longer optional upgrades. They are the minimum threshold for catching AI-generated identities before they enter the transaction.Some firms are already moving to automated verification workflows that combine biometric checks, liveness detection, and AML screening at the point of onboarding. A detailed breakdown of how identity verification works in UK property transactions covers the mechanics of what that process looks like in practice.What Regulators Are DoingThe regulatory response is accelerating but uneven.The SRA updated its AML sectorial risk assessment in March 2024 and issued explicit deepfake warnings to conveyancers. The NCA and the Law Society launched a joint campaign against payment diversion fraud in October 2025. HM Land Registry enhanced its Property Alert service, which notifies registered owners when activity is detected on their title. The UK Government passed the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, establishing a legislative basis for certified digital identity services.These are necessary steps. They are not sufficient. The enforcement timeline is measured in years. The AI tooling timeline is measured in months. Fraudsters are iterating faster than the regulatory framework can respond, which means the practical burden of prevention falls on the firms conducting the transactions.If you own property, register for HMLR's Property Alert service. It is free and sends notifications when someone attempts to change the register on your title.If you are a solicitor, estate agent, or conveyancer, the SRA's guidance is clear: do not rely on video calls alone for identity verification. Implement biometric checks with liveness detection. Cross-reference identity documents against issuing authority databases. Run sanctions and PEP screening on every party to the transaction. Document every step.The technology to prevent AI-driven property fraud exists. The question is whether the sector adopts it before the next £59 million in fraudulent applications becomes £590 million.

item.title: A Researcher's Framework for Evaluating LLM Outputs: Beyond Vibes and Gut Feelings
item.author: Olanrewaju Fatoye
item.content:encoded: Most teams evaluate LLMs using gut feeling, which leads to systems that impress in demos but fail in production. This article introduces a practical four-pillar framework for reliable LLM evaluation: define task-specific quality criteria, avoid over-reliance on single benchmarks, combine automated, human, and LLM-based evaluation methods, and treat evaluation as a continuous process. The takeaway is simple—rigorous, structured evaluation isn’t optional; it’s the difference between AI that looks good and AI that actually works.

item.title: The Missing Layer That Keeps Presto Clusters From Collapsing
item.author: Hitarth Trivedi
item.content:encoded: \ One night while I was on call for our Presto clusters, a pager alert woke me up. Error rates were climbing rapidly across several clusters that supported a major analytics workload. Queries were failing, dashboards were timing out, and engineers were starting to notice.At first nothing about the incident looked obvious. The failing queries weren’t coming from a single user or a single service. They were spread across different teams and even across different clusters.That ruled out the usual suspects. There was no obvious infrastructure failure and no single workload flooding the system.So I started digging through the query logs looking for a pattern.Eventually a pattern emerged that explained everything.That investigation reinforced something I’ve learned repeatedly while operating large query clusters. When shared data platforms start struggling, the problem is rarely storage capacity or compute power. More often the issue is simply that too many heavy queries arrive at the same time and begin competing for the same resources.That is precisely the kind of problem query governance is designed to handle. And yet, despite its importance, governance almost never appears in the architecture diagrams people use to describe modern data platforms.The Night a Cluster Started FailingOnce the alert came in, I started digging into the failures. At first the situation looked confusing. Queries were failing across several clusters that supported the same analytics workload, but the errors weren’t tied to any single user or service.That made the investigation tricky. If one team had pushed a bad query or a service had suddenly flooded the cluster, the pattern would have been obvious. Instead the failures were scattered across different users and reports. I checked whether a single cluster was overloaded or misconfigured, but the errors were appearing across multiple clusters, which ruled that out quickly.So I looked at the problem from a different angle and grouped the failing queries by query fingerprint instead of by user or cluster. A fingerprint captures the structural shape of a query rather than its literal SQL text, allowing queries with different filters or parameters to be grouped together.That was the turning point.A large number of failing queries shared the same structural pattern even though the SQL text itself looked slightly different each time. When I inspected the execution plan, the issue became clear. A report query had recently been modified and the new version was scanning dramatically more data than before. Each instance of the query was reading close to 70 terabytes of data.Running one query like that would already be expensive, but the real problem was concurrency. At peak, more than 50 instances of the same fingerprint were running simultaneously across the clusters. Individually the queries were heavy but manageable. Together they overwhelmed the system.Compute resources became saturated, queue times increased, and the clusters gradually moved into a degraded state. Once that happened even unrelated queries began failing alongside the problematic ones.The reason I was able to trace the issue back quickly was because our governance framework tracked queries by fingerprint. Once the fingerprint was identified, it was straightforward to trace the workload back to the report owner and understand what had changed.Without that governance layer, the investigation would have taken much longer.The Architecture Diagram Everyone ShowsIncidents like this one reveal a gap in how we usually describe data platforms.Most architecture diagrams focus on the core components of the analytics stack. Data lives in a distributed storage layer, often stored in columnar formats such as Parquet. Compute clusters read that data and execute distributed tasks, while a query engine translates SQL into execution plans that run across the cluster.Systems such as PrestoDB Foundation, Trino Software Foundation, and Apache Spark follow this general pattern.This model works well when only a small number of users are running queries. But once hundreds of teams begin sharing the same compute clusters, something else becomes necessary.You need a way to control how queries enter the system.That missing layer is query governance.The Missing Layer: Query GovernanceIn real deployments there is another layer sitting between users and the query engine. Its role is straightforward: control how workloads enter the cluster so that no single workload can overwhelm shared resources.\ This layer enforces the guardrails that keep shared systems stable. It decides how many queries a workload can run at the same time, which queries should wait in a queue, and which workloads should receive priority when the cluster is busy.Without those controls, clusters become vulnerable to perfectly normal workloads. A large backfill job can launch hundreds of queries within minutes. A reporting system can refresh dashboards across many datasets. A slightly modified query can suddenly scan far more data than expected.Individually these workloads are reasonable. Together they can overwhelm a shared cluster.Governance exists to keep that from happening.Most governance frameworks rely on a small set of practical guardrails that keep shared clusters from becoming unstable. Over time, I’ve found that each of these plays a distinct role in protecting the system.Quotas are usually the first line of defense. They limit how many queries a workload can run concurrently so that a single team cannot consume the entire cluster. Without quotas, even a well-intentioned workload can quickly monopolize shared resources.Admission control acts as the entry gate. It decides when new queries are allowed to enter the system, often placing them in queues when the cluster is already under pressure. This prevents sudden bursts of traffic from overwhelming the system all at once.Workload classification adds prioritization. Not all queries are equal. Critical pipelines and scheduled jobs often need to run reliably, while exploratory queries can tolerate delays. By separating workloads into tiers, the system can ensure that important jobs continue to run even during peak load.Query fingerprinting provides visibility into workload patterns. Instead of looking at individual queries, it allows the system to identify groups of structurally similar queries. In the incident I described earlier, this is exactly what allowed me to cut through the noise. While the failures appeared random across users and clusters, grouping queries by fingerprint immediately revealed that a single query pattern was responsible for the spike.Resource-based controls such as CPU quotas and query gating add another layer of protection. These mechanisms enforce limits on how much compute a workload can consume and can block or delay problematic queries when necessary.Taken together, these guardrails form a layered defense. No single mechanism is enough on its own, but combined they prevent any one workload from dominating shared compute resources and keep the system stable under pressure.Why Governance Matters More Than We AdmitLooking back at that incident, what struck me most was how ordinary the root cause turned out to be. Nothing had crashed. No component had failed. The cluster simply received more heavy queries than it could absorb at the same time.The modified report query was not unusual. It was a perfectly reasonable query that just happened to scan far more data than before. When more than fifty copies of that workload started running concurrently, the system gradually slipped into a degraded state and began rejecting other queries.That experience reinforced something I’ve learned repeatedly while operating shared data platforms. Stability rarely depends on the query engine alone. It depends on the guardrails that control how workloads enter the system.Storage lets us keep data. Query engines let us analyze it.Governance is what prevents the system from collapsing under its own success.I discussed some of these governance challenges and design decisions in more detail in a talk about operating large Presto clusters:

item.title: Lexi-9-Omega: Why the Next Great AI Won’t Just Answer Questions — It Will Engineer Reality
item.author: Andrew Westrum
item.content:encoded: Lexi-9-Omega imagines AI beyond chatbots: a structural intelligence for engineering, simulation, reverse engineering, and system design.

item.title: Happy Jewelers Introduces a More Accessible Approach to Luxury Watch Collecting
item.author: Jon Stojan Journalist
item.content:encoded: As the luxury watch-making business and market continue evolving, transparency and accessibility are becoming equally significant as history and craftsmanship. , a family-owned jewelry store operating from Fullerton, California, is taking the changing trends into consideration as they begin auctioning high-quality watches on in a manner that reflects current times but also maintains a level of authenticity.A New Kind of Auction ExperienceLuxury watch auctions are often considered highly formal events, especially for highly sought-after watches, and not everyone is allowed to participate. But Happy Jewelers has identified an opportunity to change everything through a platform with an established community of collectors. “There are platforms like eBay that have become reliable sources for collecting fans," the team argues. "We will thus be able to market our luxury watches to a larger audience."By controlling every auction, Happy Jewelers can conduct live sessions with detailed product presentations and buyer interaction.Opening the Door to More CollectorsBy implementing a $1 starting bid system, is opening the luxury watch-collecting experience to more individuals. The auctions themselves plan to cater to both novices and professionals while maintaining each product’s value. Watches from brands such as Cartier, Rolex, and Tudor still have strong market demand, reflected in their final prices. The only difference is how customers can access them.Another factor that adds momentum to the auction is inventory turnover. With one-off items being introduced into Happy Jeweler's inventory, buyers now have an opportunity to find something unique.Trust, Transparency, and Real-Time AccessConfidence continues to play a key role in every costly transaction, particularly online transactions. Happy Jewelers maintains its store policies by guaranteeing that each watch is certified and sourced through its established connections. "The buyers are getting information straight from the individuals who understand these watches," the team says, highlighting the importance of open lines of communication throughout the auction process.In addition, detailed explanations, precise condition reports, and open dialogue enhance transparency. Bidders will be able to better understand each item because they won't need to rely solely on photographs to make their decisions.Bridging Physical Retail and Digital ReachHappy Jewelers’ move into online auctions reflects a broader shift in luxury retail, where convenience and connection are expected to coexist. The brand’s history as a brick-and-mortar business continues to shape its approach to this space. Rather than separating digital and in-person experiences, the goal is to extend the same level of service into a new format. Clients who may never visit the showroom still receive the same guidance, education, and access to curated inventory.Shaping the Future of Watch CollectingAs technology continues to reshape how luxury goods are bought and sold, platforms like eBay are playing a larger role in expanding access. Happy Jewelers’ auction strategy reflects that, offering a model that blends expertise with accessibility. The interactive nature of the live auction keeps it interesting, while the changing collection ensures customers will not run out of new jewelry to explore. Buyers will also have the opportunity to interact with experts in real time to learn more about the watches.“The best way to experience our auctions is to join early, engage, and ask questions,” the team notes. “That real-time interaction allows buyers to bid with confidence.”With Happy Jewelers’ new online auctioning approach, they are opening their doors to change in how luxury watches are experienced, one that values openness, education, and connection alongside tradition.:::tip This story was distributed as a release by Jon Stojan under HackerNoon’s Business Blogging Program.

item.title: What If the Next Killer Device Isn’t a Phone — But a Memory Upgrade?
item.author: Andrew Westrum
item.content:encoded: Modern technology breaks short-term memory. A new class of AI wearables could help people preserve thoughts before they disappear.

item.title: New Report Finds Some Babies Spend Up To Eight Hours a Day on Screens
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
fjo3 shares a report from The Times: More than two-thirds of babies under two use screens, a report has found, and some are exposed for up to eight hours a day. Nearly a third of newborns were found to be watching screens for more than three hours a day, while almost 20 percent of infants of four to 11 months used screens for more than an hour a day. The report comes after the government issued guidance that children under two do not use screens at all, apart from communal activities such as video-calling relatives.
In a review of the current research, researchers found evidence linking screen time to poorer outcomes for children, including an increased risk of obesity, short-sightedness, sleep and behavioural difficulties, and later challenges with friendships. [...] The research also revealed why children and parents use screens, with families reporting children doing so for educational purposes, entertainment, play and to communicate and bond with others. Parents, meanwhile, used screens to occupy or distract children, which helped caregivers to complete domestic duties, paid employment and other caring responsibilities. Nearly a quarter of parents -- 23.6 percent -- either had no childcare or were not aware of the government's early years offer.

item.title: $700 Million Says Spain Will Win the 2026 World Cup — But France Is Closing Fast
item.author: Lujan Odera
item.content:encoded:
Polymarket has attracted over $700 million in bets on the 2026 FIFA World Cup winner — making it the largest sporting prediction market ever hosted on the platform — while Spain (17.2%) and France (15.2%) emerge as clear favourites ahead of England, Argentina, and Brazil.Dark horse nations Norway and Japan are both priced at 2.3% to win the tournament, ahead of established contenders like Belgium (1.8%) and Morocco (1.8%), signaling that traders believe emerging talent pipelines may disrupt traditional football hierarchies.Individual markets are driving engagement beyond the main winner board, with Kylian Mbappé favoured for the Golden Boot at 17%, Messi at 92% to play despite injury concerns, and the first-ever World Cup Final halftime show creating a new cultural betting frontier.The 2026 FIFA World Cup prediction market continues to hover around the major footballing nations picking up the world’s most prestigious sporting event prize. This comes even as the latest edition is set to become the first ever to feature 48 countries, in an expanded format that aims to increase competition during the event. Argentina and France – last edition’s finalists – rank firmly in the top 5 countries predicted to win the World Cup, alongside Spain, England, and Brazil, who close out the top five.Other footballing giants also show promise, including Cristiano Ronaldo’s Portugal, Germany, and the Netherlands, who have in recent times each reached at least the semi-finals stage, with Germany winning the 2014 FIFA World Cup and the Netherlands losing out narrowly to Argentina on penalties in 2022.Polymarket has attracted over $700 million in the 2026 FIFA World Cup Winner market, becoming the biggest sporting prediction market on the site, while Kalshi pales in comparison with in its contracts.2026 FIFA World Cup Race FrontrunnersPolymarket’s 2026 FIFA World Cup winner board shows Spain as the favourites with 17.2%, with last year’s finalists, France, a close second at 15.2%, England at 10.9%, Lionel Messi’s Argentina, and the current holders are at 9%, with Brazil closing out the top-five at 8.5%. 
 
 Portugal narrowly beats Germany with 7.3% of bettors leaning towards the Iberian nation and 5.3% siding with Germany. The Netherlands, which has been to the past three quarterfinals, is in eighth place with a narrow 3.3%. The clear story of the board, however, is the relatively high hopes on potential dark horses Norway and Japan, who are tied at 2.3%, ahead of Belgium and African champions Morocco, both at 1.8%. 
 
 Colombia is at 2% while the hosting nations, the USA and Mexico, are tied at 1.2% to close out the top 15. \Kalshi’s board tells a similar story: Spain and France are the favourites, at 17.2% and 16.9% respectively, with England and Brazil close behind at 12.1% and 9.7% respectively. While Spain, led by the talents of Lamine Yamal, has been a consistent contender to win it all, France saw its bets rise at the tail end of March, when players such as Michael Olise, Ryan Cherki, and Ballon d'Or winner Ousmane Dembélé picked up blistering form for their clubs as well as their win over Brazil during the international break. 
 
 The market remains wide open and active enough that traders can still price multiple paths to the winner in case of dips in form, injuries, or exclusion of their star players when the final squads are announced in the coming month.Win Your Group, Better Your OddsThe FIFA 2026 World Cup consists of 12 groups (Group A-L), with four teams each battling against each other, with the top 2 team in each group and the 8 best placed third finishers advancing to the next round. 
 Group I features France, Senegal, and Norway, alongside Iraq (effectively one of the toughest groups), with star players such as Kylian Mbappé, Erling Haaland, and Sadio Mané set to take part. On Polymarket’s board, France is heavily favoured to win the group at 71%, Norway at 22%, Senegal at 6%, and Iraq with a negligible 0.2%.Group by group, these are the leading candidates to emerge on top once the group stage is complete:| Group | Winner | Group | | |----|----|----|----| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |The USA is the only country predicted to finish the tournament higher than other countries in their group (Turkey), while not being the clear favourite to win their group. Turkey leads group D with 40%, with only a low 0.7% change at conquering it all, while the USA has a 39% chance to win their group, with a 1.2% chance of winning the tournament.Individual, Political, and Cultural Markets Pick Up PaceOne of the biggest reasons that the 2026 FIFA World Cup is driving hundreds of millions in volumes is that traders are looking to carve out individual market-specific bets. Polymarket has live markets for which continent will win the 2026 FIFA World Cup, trading with $2 million in placed bets. Europe is the strong favourite at 73%, South America at 21%, driven by Argentina and Brazil as favourites to win it, and Africa and North America at 2.9% and 2.1% respectively.\ Kalshi’s Golden Boot race winner places France’s Kylian Mbappe (17%) and England’s Harry Kane (12%) as the favourites, with Spanish teenage sensation Lamine Yamal and his counterpart Mikel Oyarzabal tied at 8%. Cristiano Ronaldo and Lionel Messi are tied at 4%, behind Argentina’s Julian Alvarez (5%) and Brazil’s Raphinha (5%), who boosted up the charts following a confirmation that he will be fit to take part in the tournament. 
 Other interesting individual markets include Neymar being called up to the final Brazil World Cup squad, currently showing a 36% chance of him going to the tournament. Lionel Messi playing at the World Cup is almost a forgone conclusion, at 92%, barring an injury or exclusion from the squad. Finally, one of the most heated debates surrounding the World Cup is if FIFA will remove Iran from competing in the competition, with 96% believing the Iranian Men’s national football team will be at the event.An interesting addition to this year’s tournament is the World Cup Final halftime show, similar to the Super Bowl, which will feature a prominent artist performing in front of 4 billion projected viewers. While the market depth for this board is wanting, several artists have been rumoured to perform, including The Weekend, Bruno Mars, Coldplay, Drake, Dua Lipa, Shakira, Beyoncé, Eminem, Burna Boy, among others. Polymarkets has gone live, giving fans a chance to predict the likely performers during the first-ever World Cup Final halftime performance.What's your take — are Spain really the favourites, or is this France's tournament to lose?

item.title: Musk Testifies OpenAI Was Created As Nonprofit To Counter Google
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
Elon Musk testified on day two of his trial against OpenAI, saying he helped create the company as a nonprofit counterweight to Google and would not have backed it if the goal had been private profit. CNBC reports: Musk on Tuesday was the first witness called to testify in the trial. He spoke about his upbringing, his many companies, his role in founding OpenAI and his understanding of its structure. Musk said in his testimony that he was not opposed to the creation of a small for-profit subsidiary, "as long as the tail didn't wag the dog." Musk said he was motivated to start OpenAI to serve as a counterweight to Google. He got the idea after an argument he had with Google co-founder Larry Page, who called Musk a "speciesist for being pro-human," he testified. "I could have started it as a for profit and I chose not to," Musk said on the stand.
Earlier, attorneys for Musk and OpenAI presented their opening arguments to the jury. Musk's lead trial lawyer, Steven Molo, delivered the opening statement for the Tesla and SpaceX CEO. OpenAI lawyer William Savitt gave the opening statement for the AI company, Altman and Brockman. OpenAI has characterized Musk's lawsuit as a baseless "harassment campaign." The company said Monday in a post on X that it "can't wait to make our case in court where both the truth and the law are on our side."
During his testimony on Tuesday, Musk repeatedly emphasized that he founded OpenAI to serve as a counterweight to Google. He said he got the idea after an argument about AI safety with Google co-founder Larry Page, who Musk said called him "a speciesist for being pro-human." Musk said he was concerned Page was not taking AI safety seriously, so he wanted there to be an nonprofit, open source alternative to Google. "I could have started it as a for profit and I chose not to," Musk said on the stand. Further reading: Elon Musk and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman Head To Court

item.title: How one venture firm is investing in an increasingly fragmented world
item.author: Tim De Chant
item.content:encoded: Geopolitical turmoil has made venture investing challenging, leading Kompas VC to carve out a niche in startups focused on the physical world.

item.title: The Secretary Of Health & Human Services Doesn’t Believe In The Foundation Of Modern Medicine
item.author: Timothy Geigner
item.content:encoded: We discussed RFK Jr.’s recent appearance before Congress, where he bravely declared that the current measles outbreak in America has absolutely nothing to do with him, despite that definitely not being true. But, unsurprisingly, that wasn’t the only craziness that Kennedy put on display in the hearing.The Secretary of HHS doesn’t believe in the foundational theory that powers modern medicine. Read that again. It’s an insane sentence, the sort that should be fiction. What we’re talking about here is the germ theory of disease, which is the accepted science when it comes to how many diseases infect and spread through pathogens. We mentioned in a post last year, which was chiefly about how Kennedy decided to take his grandkids swimming in a creek filled with poop, that he had also written in a 2021 book that he doesn’t believe in germ theory, and instead believes in what he incorrectly labels “miasma theory”.It’s one thing to write something in a book as we were mired in a global pandemic. But Kennedy both admitted that he doesn’t believe in germ theory, and defended that belief, before Congress. In the hearing on Wednesday, Sanders called attention to Kennedy’s denial of germ theory while raising one of Kennedy’s shaky arguments for debunking. In opening statements, Sanders warned Kennedy that he wanted to question the “things that you have written which call in doubt the very existence of the germ theory.”Sanders pointed out a 2024 study led by the World Health Organization and published in The Lancet that found that since 1974, vaccines had saved an estimated 154 million lives, including 146 million children under the age of 5—or, as WHO put it, vaccines saved the equivalent of six lives every minute of every year over the past 50 years.“My question is a simple one,” Sanders said, “do you still believe that one of the central tenets of the germ theory, that vaccines sharply reduce infant mortality, is quote-unquote simply untrue?”Kennedy first did what he always does: try to tell you that the experts and studies have no idea what they’re talking about, or are hopelessly corrupted tools of industry. He does this so often that you can set your watch by it. If a study agrees with him, it’s a good study. If it doesn’t, it’s bad. He’s more like Trump than any of us realized. Then he launched into his own justification and offered up a 2000 study that he claimed demonstrated that it was improved nutrition and sanitation that reduced childhood deaths this century, and explicitly medicines like vaccines. Unfortunately for Kennedy, Bill Cassidy piped up with a, oh, let’s call it a minor correction.The study by Guyer notes that sanitation, among other public health strategies introduced in the first half of the 20th century, drove major declines in mortality. But, as Cassidy noted during the hearing, it’s not all that the study found. Cassidy looked up the studies Kennedy raised and read through them during the hearing.The Guyer study highlighted that vaccination did not become widely used until after the middle of the century, thus it cannot account for mortality declines prior to that. But it concluded, as Cassidy read out loud at the hearing:The reductions in vaccine-preventable diseases, however, are impressive. In the early 1920s, diphtheria accounted for about 175,000 cases annually and pertussis for nearly 150,000 cases; measles accounted for about half a million annual cases before the introduction of vaccine in the 1960s. Deaths from these diseases have been virtually eliminated, as have deaths from Haemophilus influenzae, tetanus, and poliomyelitis.Kennedy tried again, with another study, but Cassidy pointed out that it had the same issue as Kennedy’s first: it measured data from the beginning of the century to the early 1970s. Many of the vaccines Kennedy rails against had barely been out during the period the study analyzed, or in many cases hadn’t come out at all. Speaking specifically to the measles vaccine, released in 1963, Cassidy said:“There’s 3.5 million cases of measles per year before the vaccine came along and about 550 deaths, and then the vaccine took those to less than 100 [cases] and like zero deaths,” Cassidy said. “So a tremendous impact of the vaccination.”The problem with Cassidy is that he’s acting like he’s trying to convince Kennedy to change his mind on this. He’s not going to. Not ever. He’s made that clear.So impeach him or convince Trump to make Kennedy his next cabinet firing. That’s all that’s left to do. Because we certainly cannot continue having someone run HHS who doesn’t believe in the very baseline theory for medicine.

item.author: Michael Larabel

This library is used by games from Counter-Strike to Dota 2 and since its public open-source drop has been picked up elsewhere. Finally after going nearly four years without a new version, GameNetworkingSockets v1.5 dropped today...

item.title: Electrical Current Might Be the Key To a Better Cup of Coffee
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded:
An anonymous reader quotes a report from Ars Technica: University of Oregon chemist Christopher Hendon loves his coffee -- so much so that studying all the factors that go into creating the perfect cuppa constitutes a significant area of research for him. His latest project: discovering a novel means of measuring the flavor profile of coffee simply by sending an electrical current through a sample beverage. The results appear in a new paper published in the journal Nature Communications.
[...] The coffee industry typically uses a method for measuring the refractive index of coffee -- i.e., how light bends as it travels through the liquid -- to determine strength, but it doesn't capture the contribution of roast color to the overall flavor profile. So for this latest study, Hendon decided to focus on roast color and beverage strength, the two variables most likely to affect the sensory profile of the final cuppa. His solution turned out to be quite simple. Hendon repurposed an electrochemical tool called a potentiostat, typically used to test battery and fuel cell performance. Hendon used the tool to measure how electricity interacted with the liquid. He found that this provided a better measurement of the flavor profile. He even tested it on four different samples of coffee beans and successfully identified the distinctive signature of a batch that had failed the roaster's quality-control process.
Granted, one's taste in coffee is fairly subjective, so Hendon's goal was not to achieve a "perfect" cup but to give baristas a simple tool to consistently reproduce flavor profiles more tailored to a given customer's taste. "It's an objective way to make a statement about what people like in a cup of coffee," said Hendon. "The reason you have an enjoyable cup of coffee is almost certainly that you have selected a coffee of a particular roast color and extracted it to a desired strength. Until now, we haven't been able to separate those variables. Now we can diagnose what gives rise to that delicious cup." Outside of his latest electrical-current experiment, Christopher Hendon's coffee research has shown that espresso can be made more consistently by modeling extraction yield -- how much coffee dissolves into the final drink -- and controlling water flow and pressure.
He also found that static electricity from grinding causes fine coffee particles to clump, which disrupts brewing. The solution: adding a small squirt of water to beans before grinding (known as the Ross droplet technique) to reduce that static, cut clumping and waste, and lead to a stronger, more consistent espresso.

item.title: Apple Vision Pro Used In World-First Cataract Surgery
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded: Apple's Vision Pro has been used in what's described as the world's first cataract surgery performed with the headset. MacRumors reports: [New York opthalmologist] Dr. Eric Rosenberg of SightMD completed the initial procedure in October 2025 and has since performed hundreds of additional cases using ScopeXR, a surgical platform he co-developed for Apple's mixed reality device. ScopeXR streams live feeds from 3D digital surgical microscopes directly into the Vision Pro, which lets the surgeon view the operative field in stereoscopic 3D while overlaying preoperative diagnostic data. The platform also supports real-time remote collaboration, allowing surgeons to virtually join procedures and see exactly what the operating surgeon sees. "We are now able to bring the world's best surgeon into any operating room, at any hour, from anywhere on the planet," said Dr. Rosenberg in a company press release. "From residents performing their first cases to surgeons facing unexpected complications, this technology democratizes access to expertise and that will save vision."

item.title: Tech Lobbyists Hard At Work Undermining Proposed Alaska ‘Right To Repair’ Law
item.author: Karl Bode
item.content:encoded: Alaska could be up next. Two versions of a new right to repair law are winding their way through the Alaska state House and Senate. The bills would amend the Alaska Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act, requiring tech hardware manufacturers to make parts, tools or software needed for repairs available to independent service providers and consumers.“In a lot of ways, this is a deeply conservative bill in the sense that for most of the 20th century, you could fix the stuff you bought, and the parts would be available, because it was another revenue stream for the businesses,” said Anchorage Democratic Sen. Forrest Dunbar, the sponsor of the Senate bill.”As is also always the case, hardware vendors from a variety of sectors (agricultural, medical, tech, consumer hardware) are lobbying against Alaska’s proposal, falsely claiming that easier, more affordable repairs constitute a privacy and security threat to the public. TechNet (a lobbying coalition that includes Dell, Apple, Amazon, Google, Nvidia, and Verizon), for example, is trying to convince the Alaska state legislature that everything is working fine currently, and that fixing anything would make consumers less safe. Apparently because truly independent repair professionals are too incompetent if they don’t have big corporate oversight: “TechNet wrote that the bill would erode the current system where manufacturers work with authorized repair service providers, and that these agreements “ensure that technicians have the appropriate training, access to safe repair procedures, and the qualifications necessary to protect both the device and the consumer.”TechNet is also trying to claim that the Alaska bill is “misaligned with language of right-to-repair bills from other states” such as New York. Granted they would say that, given that after New York passed its bill, tech lobbyists convinced NY Governor Kathy Hochul to water that states’s bill down to the point of uselessness. The concern now is that lobbyists successfully manage to water the Alaska bill down so badly that it ultimately becomes similarly useless.Something that’s broadly not mentioned in coverage of right to repair: while eight states have passed right to repair laws in recent years, not of those states has actually managed to actively enforce it, despite no shortage of bad behavior by companies looking to secure repair monopolies. That’s something that needs to change if the movement is to have any serious impact.
